key_insight: Coordination-aware tree-ensemble surrogate models can predict multiple spinel oxide/sulfide/selenide properties well, but performance depends strongly on the target and data regime.
engineering_relevance: This offers a fast screening tool for thermodynamic stability and magnetism in spinel materials, helping prioritize candidates before expensive first-principles calculations or experiments.
method: 320 cubic spinel entries were curated from the Materials Project across nitrides, oxides, sulfides, and selenides.
Cations were grouped by CrystalNN coordination numbers into tetrahedral-like and octahedral-like sites, not by fixed element identity.
Tree-ensemble surrogates were trained for formation energy, hull distance, band gap, total magnetization, and metallicity using grouped cross-validation and holdout testing.
evidence_signal: Over repeated grouped holdouts, the best models achieved MAE of 0.121 eV/atom for formation energy, 0.048 eV/atom for hull distance, 1.27 μB/fu for magnetization, and 0.85 accuracy for metallicity, while band-gap prediction did not beat a trivial baseline on the 19-member non-metal set.
watch_item: Band-gap results are limited by small sample size and semi-local DFT label quality, so the model should not be overinterpreted for insulating spinels.
paper_title: Coordination-Resolved Surrogate Models for Thermodynamic Stability, Band Gaps, and Magnetic Moments of Spinel Oxides, Sulfides, and Selenides
